sentences.text: Melk was difficult.
But as I told you before, I no longer felt things.
I mean, the people who thought, or who worried, or who in some way behaved as humans died.
They became-- you heard about Muselmanns.
And people who became Muselmann, they just had so much time left.
They died.
And people who became like this are people who usually strived to maintain some form of a humanity.
That was impossible.
So the concentration, the survival, the instinct to survive had to be paramount, only aware of the present.

Food, some form of warmth, some form of sleep-- whatever is possible, because they never let us rest at night either-- and in some way, controlling the lice, which was a terrible thing, living with lice.
I mean, the whole thing's, all day long, like this-- this type of a feeling.
I can hardly remember now.
But the feeling of hunger, the feeling of lice, and the-- the-- the things which you cannot describe or-- or see in the movie, the smell or the risk of hunger [INAUDIBLE].
But these were the concerns that we have to live with.
Only concentrate on that, and get through at any price, at any cost, and nobody else counted.
Survival had to be individual.
They had the hospital there, which they put people in there, but before they were healed, if they didn't die there, they would put them back to work.
And then they were never ready.
They usually couldn't take it, and they would die.
I remember the winter time, there was a man came with our group, just came from the hospital-- a tall, distinguished looking Hungarian Jew.
He just came from a hospital night shift.
And he could hardly walk.
He had a pair of new wooden shoes.
At that point, my shoes collapsed-- tore apart.
So I had the-- the bottom wood part, and cement sack, which I wrapped with wires to keep it together.
And this is how I walked with the snow and all this.
He had the wooden shoes, which were new.
And I knew he wasn't going to last, so I hung around him.
I became his friend.
I supported him, and so on.
I was waiting for him to collapse.
And before he left, he collapsed.
Orders were given to march.
That's when he collapsed on the ground.
And the first thing I did is run to him and start taking his shoes off.
Which is, I started yelling, my shoes.
You can't live without shoes.
So the kapos go first, and first there was that beating.
They didn't ask questions.
First they beat.
And then we find out what's going on.
And he found out I wanted his shoes, and he was going back to the hospital.
He beat him up for, for refusing to give me the shoes, and I got the shoes.
And the fact to me, it was perfectly proper.
He was going to die anyway.
What does he need his shoes for?
But I was going to get them because it would save my life.
So this is how things were done.
So the conditions in Melk-- we had the large contingents of Russian prisoners of war there.
And so there were not just Jews, but all kinds of people from all nationalities.
And they were all animals.
And I don't know that there were-- maybe they [INAUDIBLE] it.
But in our surroundings, all people were just out to survive.
And being an animal is as close as you could describe a person.
The only difference was there was a barrack in the camp which was surrounded by its own wire.
So the people who were in there, they couldn't go out and mix with the others, and nobody could go in.
So I asked, who are these people?
Must be very dangerous if they have a camp within the camp of this particular group.
Says, who are they?
They are-- they are Jehovah's Witnesses.
They're all Germans mostly, and they were locked up because they wouldn't go along with Hitler.
They wouldn't serve in the army.
I says, why did they lock them up?
He says, because otherwise, they will go and preach to others.
They are considered too dangerous for that.
But what they told me is, these people were the only ones who were not victims because they were told by signing a statement denouncing their religion was enough to set them free.
And they wouldn't go.
And this is something which was totally unusual, because any one of us would-- can you imagine signing a piece of paper to get out?
So I mean, it was, there was a talk of the camp.
And they were there, most of them, going back to 1933-34.
Anyway, so, these were-- these people were the ones-- who were-- they supported one another.
They always had good positions in the kitchen, or working for the Germans.
So the Germans trusted them for babysitters, or maybe as barbers.
They wouldn't cut their throats , and so on.
So as a result, they had good positions.
And these people were known to always support one another, help one another.
They was-- they were human.
The only humans in the camp really stood out and made a powerful impression.
None.
No, none.
I just knew of them, and I saw them in the distance.
And I didn't know anything about them.
All I knew about their religion is somebody told me they don't believe in the Pope.
[LAUGHS] Sum total.
But this doesn't count.
I mean, the fact was these strange people who are sitting in the camp because, they-- I guess, they could get out.
Why don't they?
But the contrast between the people who maintained sanity and the rest of us was so big, it really emphasized just how different we had become.
Melk in April 12th was my birthday.
I was 17 years old-- April 12th, 1945.
This was a day Roosevelt died, I found out later.
We stood for 24 hours at attention in appelplatz because somebody escaped.
I mean, you couldn't move [INAUDIBLE].
So I remember that day, and the next day, April 13, was a Friday.
I wasn't superstitious, but that sort of stuck in my mind.
We went off on a march, a death march from Melk to a city in the mountain, or a camp in the mountain, Ebensee.
And well, I mean, you know about death marches.
I mean, whoever fell behind then got shot.
No food on the way.
Stopped-- I retraced it with my wife, sort of retraced the death march-- the locations, rather.
Driving through the area, hoping that somehow, is gonna bring back some of those memories and some form of a cathartic, emotional reaction.
It didn't work.
I just-- I was so locked up, I couldn't bring any of these things back.
It meant nothing, nothing.
Going from Mauthausen, going to Ebensee, means nothing.
Anyway, wound up in Ebensee.
About six days or so.
Friend only in the sense of sharing a common experience, and people you talk to or people you knew.
But friend-- I mean, nobody could help anybody else.
And nobody would support one another.
I mean, this was in the beginning.
People would be dragged for a while, but they couldn't fall behind.
And then both would be shot.
So people dropped and just kept walking.
And then you heard a shot, in the back, and-- and that's it.
But you see, the feelings and emotions were numbed.
They doesn't exist.
The whole thing is another step, another step, and another step.
And everything else just didn't exist.
We did engage, starting in Melk, in Mauthausen, in Melk, where the food became scarce, the fantasies.
One way of standing in appelplatz for hours sometimes is to think about food, and to go close your eyes, and go over the Friday night meal at home.
Meal by meal, bite by bite, imagining what it was like.
Or talking about food.
But that's our only dream.
And I remember-- of course, I was always-- no matter what, I never felt that I would die.
I had this feeling that I'm gonna live.
I was not ready for death, [INAUDIBLE] no matter what the conditions were.
And my dream was, just one day, if I survive, I would like to work indoors, maybe a mechanic or some type.
And then at the end of the day, I would go into the room with a bunch of lockers with keys.
I have locked locker.
And in the locker, on top, there will be a loaf of bread.
And I can sit down and eat all I want without having to worry it's gonna run out.
This was my total-- as far as my dreams [? would ?] [? dare ?] to go, but I didn't think it's gonna be like that.
So food was important.
And food was the only thing we allowed ourselves to, to have fantasies about.
Not people.
Not thinking about my parents.
Not thinking about anybody else.
Other than what happened to my brother, I didn't care.
It didn't matter.
Food, and the reality of the present.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Ebensee was tough.
We were there only from beginning-- I don't know, about April 19th or so to May 6th.
No food.
I survived because I-- this was in the forest-- and I found pieces of bark, or something like this, chewing on this, and some coal.
People began to die from diarrhea, among other things.
And I didn't realize, but I was eating some like, coal-like substance, brown coal, which apparently was-- had some affect on diarrhea.
And many things would push against the survival.
Ebensee was terrible.
We worked for to start with in a-- there was a-- a-- a rail station nearby, which was heavily bombed by the Americans.
We were digging this up.
And then, apparently, the Americans were close.
We didn't know how close the Americans were.
They wouldn't let us out of the camp.
We just sat there.
There's no food.
People began to die by the thousands of starvation.
There was a typical-- people who die of starvation begin to swell.
And the body absorbs moisture, fat.
And first of all, the eyes, the puffy cheeks.
And then, the stomach and the rest of the body-- there's a predictable course, after you reach a certain point, how long it will be before the people die.
I remember how many times we were sleeping in bunks, which are four people in a bunk-- four people went to bed at night, and two may be dead the next morning right next to you.
I began to develop the, the already the vestiges of starvation-- you know, puffy cheeks and stomach and thighs.
I have a few days left.
The-- there were Russians in there.
The bodies were-- they were accumulated so-- I'm talking Russian prisoners.
But the bodies were accumulated so fast, that they couldn't burn them.
The crematorium couldn't handle all of the bodies.
And the Russians got worried, so they started cutting out pieces of meat and trading them.
So the cannibalism started in camp as well.
Finally, the day before liberation, they called us.
We had a big gathering.
And the camp commander says that unfortunately, the Americans are approaching.
And they have to turn over the camp to the Americans.
However, they expect that there will be possibly a battle between the Germans and the Americans.
So for our safety, they wanted us to go through the tunnels that we have built until the war is over.
Well, we refused.
At that point, they couldn't force us.
I mean, most of the Germans were gone, and the guards-- we said no.
And that night, the, the tunnels blew up.
In some cases, some of camp's people actually did die in that way.
Anyway, by that time, I was already sort of losing consciousness.
I didn't know what was going on.
The next day, the Americans came.
I was lying there in the bunk.
I no longer had any feeling for that.
I mean, this-- I was too, too far gone.
And the fact the Americans came didn't make an impression.
Can you imagine that?
And then somebody said that-- that the-- somebody broke into the bakery.
There's bread.
I responded to that.
But I couldn't make it.
Anyway, the story goes, in a few days, the Americans came in, and they moved us to another place in the camp.
Not the original, further outside.
And we were all starving.
The troops that came in did not have any food supplies with them, so they raided the town, and they found the mixed supplies of canned food-- you know, pork and beans, and stuff like this.
And they issued the orders to cook and give those prisoners as much as they want.
So they came with a soup, fat, pork, and beans, and stuff like this.
They-- we lined up, and just ate it concentration camp style.
The idea was to eat it as fast as you can, gulp it down, wipe the-- the-- your soup can.
This was always soup.
There was always-- the dish was always attached by a wire to your pants.
And get back in line.
Before, this had been punishable by death.
But this here, the chances are there was enough soup, so go right ahead.
And I went back in line, got more soup.
I couldn't eat it anymore.
So I sat on the bunk all day long, watching it.
And then in the evening, more soup came in.
But I still had this soup in here, and there a layer of fat like this.
I skimmed off the fat, and I ate the soup, the cold soup, and got back in line.
They gave more.
Well, everybody did that.
And people begin to drop.
People began to drop right on the spot.
I mean, it was diarrhea, and just by the hundreds.
Because this was the wrong food.
It just killed them.
Interesting side to this story.
The Holocaust Museum in Washington now has a display called 'The Liberation.' It shows the liberation of the camps.
And the centerpiece-- I am a member, so I get the monthly periodical-- there's a picture.
This is the centerpiece, which is the big part of mural, 'Liberation of Ebensee.' And there are the prisoners standing there behind the wires, and looking out.
I never know how many people noticed if all of them, every one of them, had just a top, jacket, but no pants.
I want to write to them, let them know.
Because I wonder if they realize why they had no pants on.
Because everybody had diarrhea.
They could never make it to any place to a-- to a toilet, or anything like this.
So all of us had no pants, except to strip off, maybe somebody off a dead person, if it was clean.
This is sort of the photograph today.
I forgot all about this until I saw the picture.
And I said, oh yeah, we had no pants.
I was-- after I, I recovered from the swelling, I had diarrhea, but it didn't kill me.
However, I was a Muselmann.
A Muselmann.
Other people began to eat, and to fill out.
And those who didn't die made out very-- began to be a normal human again.
I couldn't gain weight.
I was about 60 pounds.
And I remember what the-- some of the prisoners would go is go begging of the Austrians, you know, beg for food.
They'd go from door to door, beg for food.
And they'll take me along because I was so skinny, I looked so bad, that people had no heart to refuse.
So I was used for that purpose.
There was an American camp outside, and-- You know, the-- the American camps, they eat from their mess kits, and then they have those big garbage cans, where they throw the refuse at the end.
So we used to sneak up over there to the-- on a hill.
And then when they finished eating, we would run like mad and scoop up all the garbage.
You know, the mixture.
Whatever they had-- was coffee, and cigarette butts, and everything that was there.
This was-- how we used to all call organize.
This was called organization.
Anyway, the American Red Cross came in one day and asked, is anybody sick here?
So they pointed to me.
He's sick.
And they came to me and wanted to take me to the hospital.
I absolutely refused with all [INAUDIBLE].
I wouldn't go.
Because   to me, hospital was death.
So I refused to go.
As it turned out, they were taken to a very fine hospital, and they were treated very well.
However, I stayed there and I was sick.
And I-- finally, somebody, a friend, he took me to a German doctor, forced his way in, and says, issue him a ticket to the hospital, permission to go to the hospital.
And this is how I wound up in a hospital in a-- an Austrian town.
I stayed there for a few days.
They found a spot on my lungs, and they moved me to a hospital for Luftwaffe officers who had TB.
And I was only one among the Luftwaffe officers in that hospital.
I never told them I was Jewish because I was scared.
I was surrounded by all these people.
And I began to gain weight.
I mean, I was just gaining weight at the rate, I don't know, 12 pounds, [? 13 ?] pounds a week.
I mean, just unbelievable.
And I recovered.
There are many stories which I'm not giving you at this point, because time is really running out, I guess.
This was somebody who was living in the same barracks in Ebensee, and who felt sorry for me.
And I-- I could not recover my strength or my weight.
And he felt sorry for me and took the action.
I mean, I don't know who he was.
I mean, he wasn't a friend friend.
It was somebody I knew who did it for me.
I was in that hospital, and I began to recover.
And one day, I went off for a walk by myself up in the hills, beautiful town.
And I walk up the road, and I saw two Austrian children playing.
And I just stopped right there.
I didn't know what-- what-- I was in total state of shock.
I didn't see children for years.
And to see two children playing in the street, I just didn't know what to make of it.
And they saw me stop over there.
And I-- I was wearing-- I had-- I was wearing stripes because that's all I had, striped pants.
And somebody gave me an air force-- German Air Force jacket.
This was my uniform.
And they saw me-- they must have been six years old or something.
And they yelled at me, [NON-ENGLISH].
Go away, you lousy boy, you know.
And I just turned around, and I marched right back.
I took two days to recover from this experience.
It was sort of a strange thing-- feeling of coming back to a different world.
Then they-- every once in awhile, they would take people who were healed or whatever, and they let them go.

Now, the Germans had to go through the-- go through a town, [NON-ENGLISH], and they have to go through the process of being released from the military, go through some military-- some government procedures.
They would get ration cards.
And they would get the tickets back home.
So they put them on the bus.
And this was the day when I was released as well.
So I went with them.
OK.
And then I go to the office.
And I-- [INAUDIBLE] are you a soldier?
I says no.
[INAUDIBLE] We cannot help you.
I says, where do I go?
Says, we don't know.
We only help the German soldiers.
So I would see even here, I was rejected.
[LAUGHS] I wound up working my way back to the camp in Ebensee.
In the meantime, they moved them to another location for the DP camp.
And I showed up there in the evening.
And I said, I'm back here.
I came from here, and I'd like to go back.
They says, you can't stay here.
We don't know who you are.
I says, I came back from the hospital.
How do we know?
Maybe you escaped from a jail.
We cannot take you.
Go back to the hospital and get some papers.
This kind of a thing.
Anyway, I settle back in there.
And then I began to look around who survived.
Somebody said my brother survived.
And then somebody says, your sister survived.
My brother is dead.
I went looking for the-- for my sister.
By that time, I accepted that my brother died.
But people came from Poland, and they saw my sister.
My sister was-- well, anyway, that's-- I have to cut this a little bit.
No?
Well.
I went to a town of Linz.
And there was people who came from Poland, and some who were going to Poland.
There was a big field, just like a gypsy camp, with fires burning, and people living in all kinds of-- I mean, this scene is-- it'd have to be seen to be believed.
And I worked over there, looking for anybody from Krakow, anybody who knows my sister.
I didn't find any.
But then I-- I-- since I had no possessions-- I had nothing except what I carried on my back, I decided to stay in Linz in-- in their camp called Bindermichl.
And I stayed there while writing letters.
Because somebody-- I found people who said, I know your sister.
I know that she survived.
I think that she is some place in Germany.
And I wrote the letter once to somebody who came from Germany.
This was in Austria.
And she says, your sister is in some DP camp.
I don't know the name.
So I wrote the letter, and addressed it to her DP camp-- [INAUDIBLE] there was no DP camp-- Judenlager some place in Germany.
And she got it.
I have a copy of this letter here.
It's quite a statement that I put in there when I look back.
She came back.
She came to Germany illegally because there was no way of traveling, crossing the river at night.
And came, and she found me.
And she walked into the room where I was.
I was swollen.
I mean, that time, for some reason, I was gaining weight in an abnormal way.
My face was square.
Strange.
I'm trying to recapture those feelings when I saw her, and don't think I can do that.
I cannot even remember what it felt like.
There was just total silence.
Total-- it was very difficult to come back to life again.
Anyway, we-- again, we went back to Germany.
The way it went, the American troops came aboard as you crossed the border from Austria to Germany.
And they asked you where you're going.
I said, we're going to Germany.
They took you off the train, and put you back on the Austrian side.
So the-- so the idea-- they say, where are you going, if you say you're going to Austria, then they put on the German side.
This kind of a thing.
And we-- we hid in a toilet, and we made it to Salzburg.
And from-- and from there, there was a way of bribing your way back to Germany.
We went to Landsberg.
She was a good organizer.
She was working for the Russian cart bureau over there, so we had access to all kinds of food.
Then life became normal, DP camp.
